title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: vignette walks complete bioenergetics analysis juvenile Oncorhynchus tshawytscha (Chinook salmon) using conditions representative interior Pacific Northwest lake single growing season (180 days, April–September). cover: Setting model built-parameter database Estimating daily consumption target final weight (binary search) Quantifying uncertainty via bootstrap resampling Summarising growth, energy budgets, feeding performance

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: fb4package ships built-database (fish4_parameters) covering 105 parameterisations published literature.
code:
data(fish4_parameters)  chinook_db <- fish4_parameters[["Oncorhynchus tshawytscha"]] stage      <- if ("juvenile" %in% names(chinook_db$life_stages)) {                 "juvenile"               } else {                 names(chinook_db$life_stages)[1]               }  sp_params <- chinook_db$life_stages[[stage]] sp_info   <- chinook_db$species_info sp_info$life_stage <- stage  cat("Life stage  :", stage, "
") #> Life stage  : adult cat("CEQ (consumption equation):", sp_params$consumption$CEQ, "
") #> CEQ (consumption equation): 3 cat("REQ (respiration equation):", sp_params$respiration$REQ, "
") #> REQ (respiration equation): 1

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: simulate 180-day seasonal temperature profile typical Pacific Northwest lake (April September), peak late July.
code: set.seed(42) days      <- 1:180 base_temp <- 7 + 7 * sin(pi * (days - 20) / 180)   # peak ~14 °C at day 110 temp_vec  <- pmax(2, base_temp + rnorm(180, 0, 0.4))  temp_data <- data.frame(   Day         = days,   Temperature = round(temp_vec, 2) )  cat(sprintf("Temperature range : %.1f – %.1f °C\n",             min(temp_data$Temperature), max(temp_data$Temperature))) #> Temperature range : 4.6 – 15.0 °C cat(sprintf("Mean temperature  : %.1f °C\n", mean(temp_data$Temperature))) #> Mean temperature  : 11.2 °C

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: Juvenile Chinook Pacific Northwest lakes consume primarily forage fish (alewife) summer, supplemented shrimp invertebrates spring autumn. Prey energy densities typical values literature (J/g wet weight).
code: alewife <- pmax(0, 0.55 + 0.25 * sin(pi * (days - 30) / 180)) shrimp  <- pmax(0, 0.28 - 0.10 * sin(pi * (days - 30) / 180)) inverts <- pmax(0, 1 - alewife - shrimp) total   <- alewife + shrimp + inverts  diet_props <- data.frame(   Day     = days,   Alewife = round(alewife / total, 4),   Shrimp  = round(shrimp  / total, 4),   Inverts = round(inverts / total, 4) )  prey_energy <- data.frame(   Day     = days,   Alewife = 4900,   # J/g (wet weight)   Shrimp  = 3200,   Inverts = 2600 )  cat("Diet proportions (first 3 days):\n") #> Diet proportions (first 3 days): print(head(diet_props, 3)) #>   Day Alewife Shrimp Inverts #> 1   1  0.4288 0.3285  0.2427 #> 2   2  0.4326 0.3269  0.2404 #> 3   3  0.4365 0.3254  0.2381

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: model components assembled single Bioenergetic object. Initial weight set 5 g, representing post-emergence juvenile.
code: bio_chinook <- Bioenergetic(   species_params     = sp_params,   species_info       = sp_info,   environmental_data = list(temperature = temp_data),   diet_data          = list(     proportions = diet_props,     prey_names  = c("Alewife", "Shrimp", "Inverts"),     energies    = prey_energy   ),   simulation_settings = list(initial_weight = 5, duration = 180) )  # Predator energy density: linear interpolation from 4 200 to 5 000 J/g # as the fish accumulate lipids through summer (PREDEDEQ = 1 from DB) bio_chinook$species_params$predator$ED_ini <- 4200 bio_chinook$species_params$predator$ED_end <- 5000  print(bio_chinook) #> FB4 Bioenergetic Model #> ========================= #> Species: Oncorhynchus tshawytscha (Chinook salmon (adult)) #> Setup: 5 g → 180 days #>  #> Components: #>   ✓ Parameters: 41 params (consumption, respiration, activity, sda, egestion, excretion, predator, source, notes) #>   ✓ Temperature: 180 days (4.6-15°C) #>   ✓ Diet: 3 prey species, 180 days #>  #> Status: Ready for fitting

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: Model setup dashboard: environmental diet data coverage. Seasonal temperature profile used simulation. Daily diet composition 180-day season.
code: plot(bio_chinook, type = "dashboard") plot(bio_chinook, type = "temperature", colors = "red") plot(bio_chinook, type = "diet", colors = "green")

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: ask: feeding level (p) produces final weight 40 g 180 days? standard FB4 approach target weight measured field.
code:
res_bs <- run_fb4(   x         = bio_chinook,   fit_to    = "Weight",   fit_value = 40,   strategy  = "binary_search",   verbose   = FALSE )  cat(sprintf("Estimated p-value     : %.4f
", res_bs$summary$p_value)) #> Estimated p-value     : 0.3714 cat(sprintf("Final weight          : %.1f g
", res_bs$summary$final_weight)) #> Final weight          : 40.0 g cat(sprintf("Total consumption     : %.1f g
", res_bs$summary$total_consumption_g)) #> Total consumption     : 141.2 g cat(sprintf("Simulation converged  : %s
",     res_bs$summary$converged)) #> Simulation converged  : TRUE

what: 6. Consumption with a fixed feeding level
title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: p known (e.g., bioenergetics study), use strategy = "direct_p_value" forward-simulate without fitting.
code: res_direct <- run_fb4(   x         = bio_chinook,   fit_to    = "p_value",   fit_value = 0.75,   strategy  = "direct_p_value",   verbose   = FALSE )  cat(sprintf("Final weight at p = 0.75 : %.1f g\n", res_direct$summary$final_weight)) #> Final weight at p = 0.75 : 376.0 g cat(sprintf("Total consumption        : %.1f g\n", res_direct$summary$total_consumption_g)) #> Total consumption        : 1055.3 g

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: field data include multiple final weights (e.g., sample individually tagged fish), bootstrap resampling propagates measurement variability p estimate. simulate 25 observed final weights around binary-search result, CV 8 % mimic realistic field sampling variability. Bootstrap distribution estimated p-values 95% CI.
code:
set.seed(123) n_obs           <- 25 final_wt_true   <- res_bs$summary$final_weight obs_weights     <- rnorm(n_obs, mean = final_wt_true, sd = final_wt_true * 0.08)  res_boot <- run_fb4(   x                = bio_chinook,   fit_to           = "Weight",   observed_weights = obs_weights,   strategy         = "bootstrap",   n_bootstrap      = 100,   upper            = 1,   parallel         = FALSE,   confidence_level = 0.95,   verbose          = FALSE )  cat(sprintf("p mean (bootstrap) : %.4f
", res_boot$summary$p_mean)) #> p mean (bootstrap) : 0.3711 cat(sprintf("p SD               : %.4f
", res_boot$summary$p_sd)) #> p SD               : 0.0019 cat(sprintf("95%% CI             : [%.4f, %.4f]
",             res_boot$method_data$confidence_intervals$p_ci_lower,             res_boot$method_data$confidence_intervals$p_ci_upper)) #> 95% CI             : [0.3672, 0.3747] plot(res_boot, type = "uncertainty")

=== Feeding performance ===

title: Case Study: Chinook Salmon Bioenergetics
text: estimated p ≈ 0.62 indicates juvenile Chinook consumed approximately 62 % bioenergetically predicted maximum ration 180-day growing season. gross conversion efficiency near 0.13–0.15 consistent published values salmonids temperature ranges (Deslauriers et al. 2017). energy budget plot shows respiration dominates energy expenditure (~55–60 % consumed energy), egestion excretion accounting another 20 %. remaining ~20–25 % directed towards somatic growth.

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: fb4package implements Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 (Deslauriers et al. 2017) R. model allocates consumed energy among metabolism, waste, growth produce daily estimates fish consumption weight gain. Working package follows three-step pattern: build Bioenergetic object holds species parameters, temperature, diet, initial conditions; pass run_fb4() run simulation fit feeding level; extract plot results.

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: package ships built-database covering 105 models (73 species). Retrieve Chinook salmon (Oncorhynchus tshawytscha) inspect one life stage:
code:
data(fish4_parameters) cat("Species in database:", length(fish4_parameters), "
") #> Species in database: 73 head(names(fish4_parameters), 8) #> [1] "Alosa pseudoharengus"           "Gadus morhua"                   #> [3] "Brevoortia tyrannus"            "Thymallus arcticus baicalensis" #> [5] "Clupea harengus"                "Anchoa mitchilli"               #> [7] "Hypophthalmichthys nobilis"     "Coregonus hoyi" chinook_db  <- fish4_parameters[["Oncorhynchus tshawytscha"]] stage       <- names(chinook_db$life_stages)[1] cat("Life stage used:", stage, "
") #> Life stage used: adult  sp_params   <- chinook_db$life_stages[[stage]] sp_info     <- chinook_db$species_info  # Key consumption parameters cat("CEQ:", sp_params$consumption$CEQ,     "  CA:", sp_params$consumption$CA,     "  CB:", sp_params$consumption$CB, "
") #> CEQ: 3   CA: 0.303   CB: -0.275

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: Temperature diet supplied data frames Day column.
code: days <- 1:100  # Seasonal temperature (°C) temp_data <- data.frame(   Day         = days,   Temperature = round(10 + 5 * sin(2 * pi * (days - 60) / 365), 2) )  # Two-prey diet (constant proportions, J/g energy densities) diet_data <- list(   proportions = data.frame(Day = days, Alewife = 0.7, Shrimp = 0.3),   prey_names  = c("Alewife", "Shrimp"),   energies    = data.frame(Day = days, Alewife = 4900, Shrimp = 3200) )  head(temp_data, 3) #>   Day Temperature #> 1   1        5.75 #> 2   2        5.80 #> 3   3        5.84

title: Introduction to fb4package
code: bio <- Bioenergetic(   species_params      = sp_params,   species_info        = sp_info,   environmental_data  = list(temperature = temp_data),   diet_data           = diet_data,   simulation_settings = list(initial_weight = 1800, duration = 100) )  # Required when PREDEDEQ = 2 (energy density as function of weight) bio$species_params$predator$ED_ini <- 4500 bio$species_params$predator$ED_end <- 5500  print(bio) #> FB4 Bioenergetic Model #> ========================= #> Species: Oncorhynchus tshawytscha (Chinook salmon (adult)) #> Setup: 1800 g → 100 days #>  #> Components: #>   ✓ Parameters: 41 params (consumption, respiration, activity, sda, egestion, excretion, predator, source, notes) #>   ✓ Temperature: 100 days (5.8-13.2°C) #>   ✓ Diet: 2 prey species, 100 days #>  #> Status: Ready for fitting

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: Run model constant proportion maximum consumption (p = 0.8):
code: res_direct <- run_fb4(bio, strategy = "direct", p_value = 0.8) print(res_direct) #> FB4 Simulation Results #> ========================= #> Species: Oncorhynchus tshawytscha #> Method: Direct Execution #> Duration: 100 days #>  #> RESULTS: #>   Initial weight: 1800 g #>   Final weight: 2647.37 g #>   Growth: 47.1% #>   Total consumption: 4821.23 g #>   P_value: 0.8 #>  #> FITTING: ✓ Successful

what: 4b. Binary search — fit to a target final weight

res_bs <- run_fb4(bio,                   strategy  = "binary_search",                   fit_to    = "Weight",                   fit_value = 3000)  cat("Estimated p-value :", round(res_bs$summary$p_value, 4), "
") #> Estimated p-value : 0.9095 cat("Final weight      :", round(res_bs$summary$final_weight, 1), "g

") #> Total consumption : 5771.2 g

what: 4c. MLE — uncertainty on p from observed weights
title: Introduction to fb4package
text: multiple fish weights available, strategy = "mle" returns point estimate p together standard error 95% confidence interval:
code:
set.seed(42) obs_weights <- rnorm(30, mean = 3000, sd = 80)   # 30 observed final weights  res_mle <- run_fb4(bio,                    strategy         = "mle",                    fit_to           = "Weight",                    observed_weights = obs_weights)  cat("p-value (MLE)  :", round(res_mle$summary$p_value, 4), "
") #> p-value (MLE)  : 0.9106 cat("SE             :", round(res_mle$summary$p_se,    4), "
") #> SE             : 0.0055 cat("Converged      :", res_mle$summary$converged, "
") #> Converged      : TRUE

title: Introduction to fb4package
code: set.seed(42) res_boot <- run_fb4(bio,                     strategy         = "bootstrap",                     fit_to           = "Weight",                     observed_weights = obs_weights,                     upper            = 1,                     n_bootstrap      = 100,                     parallel         = FALSE)  cat("p mean (bootstrap):", round(res_boot$summary$p_mean, 4), "\n") #> p mean (bootstrap): 0.911 cat("p SD              :", round(res_boot$summary$p_sd,   4), "\n") #> p SD              : 0.0057

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: three methods converge similar p estimates data — binary search gives point estimate, MLE bootstrap additionally quantify uncertainty: strategies use bio object (Chinook salmon, 100-day simulation). MLE bootstrap fit obs_weights; binary search fits target weight.

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: following modules implemented standalone functions yet integrated main run_fb4() loop. can called directly custom analyses connected automatically future release.

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: Three bioaccumulation models (dietary uptake, gill exchange, bioenergetics-based): calculate_contaminant_accumulation() — daily bioaccumulation process_contaminant_params() — parameter pre-processing

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: Daily nitrogen phosphorus fluxes (ingestion, retention, excretion): calculate_nutrient_balance() — daily N/P budget calculate_nutrient_efficiencies() — N P use efficiencies process_nutrient_params() — parameter pre-processing

title: Introduction to fb4package
text: Natural, fishing, predation mortality rates can processed via process_mortality_params() computed step--step calculate_mortality_reproduction(), mortality rates yet applied automatically inside run_fb4(). Note: spawning energy loss (reproductive cost) already integrated applies automatically reproduction_data supplied Bioenergetic object.

title: Exploring the Species Parameter Database
text: Every entry follows hierarchical structure:
code:
fish4_parameters[["Species name"]] ├── species_info        # taxonomic information ├── life_stages         # named list of life-stage parameter sets │   ├── juvenile        # (or adult, larval, etc.) │   │   ├── consumption │   │   ├── respiration │   │   ├── activity │   │   ├── sda │   │   ├── egestion │   │   ├── excretion │   │   └── predator    # energy density equation │   └── adult           # (if present) └── sources             # literature references # Inspect a single entry chinook <- fish4_parameters[["Oncorhynchus tshawytscha"]]  cat("=== Species info ===
") #> === Species info === print(chinook$species_info) #> $scientific_name #> [1] "Oncorhynchus tshawytscha" #>  #> $common_name #> [1] "Chinook salmon (adult)" #>  #> $family #> [1] "Salmonidae" #>  #> $order #> [1] "Salmoniformes"  cat("
=== Life stages available ===
") #>  #> === Life stages available === print(names(chinook$life_stages)) #> [1] "adult"  cat("
=== Sources ===
") #>  #> === Sources === print(chinook$sources) #> [1] "Stewart & Ibarra 1991; Plumb & Moffit 2015"

title: Exploring the Species Parameter Database
text: maximum consumption rate governed : Cmax=CA⋅WCB⋅f(T)C_{\max} = C_A \cdot W^{C_B} \cdot f(T) CA scales intercept CB weight exponent. Higher CA values indicate higher maximum ration relative body weight. Top 10 entries CA (maximum consumption coefficient)
code: # Extract CA, CB, CEQ for all entries (first life stage) param_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(fish4_parameters), function(sp) {   entry <- fish4_parameters[[sp]]   stage <- names(entry$life_stages)[1]   cons  <- entry$life_stages[[stage]]$consumption    data.frame(     Species    = sp,     Stage      = stage,     CEQ        = cons$CEQ  %||% NA,     CA         = cons$CA   %||% NA,     CB         = cons$CB   %||% NA,     CTO        = cons$CTO  %||% NA,     CTM        = cons$CTM  %||% NA,     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) }))  # Show top 10 by CA (highest maximum ration) param_table_sorted <- param_table[order(-param_table$CA, na.last = TRUE), ] knitr::kable(   head(param_table_sorted[, c("Species", "Stage", "CEQ", "CA", "CB", "CTO", "CTM")], 10),   caption = "Top 10 entries by CA (maximum consumption coefficient)",   digits  = 4 )

title: Exploring the Species Parameter Database
text: Distribution consumption temperature optima (CTO) across database entries.
code: cto_vals <- na.omit(param_table$CTO)  hist(cto_vals,      breaks = 15,      main   = "Consumption temperature optima (CTO)",      xlab   = "CTO (°C)",      ylab   = "Number of entries",      col    = "steelblue",      border = "white") abline(v = median(cto_vals), col = "tomato", lwd = 2, lty = 2) legend("topright", legend = paste("Median =", round(median(cto_vals), 1), "°C"),        col = "tomato", lty = 2, lwd = 2, bty = "n")

title: Exploring the Species Parameter Database
text: database includes three equations predator (fish) energy density: PREDEDEQ = 1 (common), user must supply ED_ini ED_end (full ED_data vector) since daily energy density varies season condition factor. See Introduction vignette example.
code: prededeq_vals <- sapply(names(fish4_parameters), function(sp) {   entry <- fish4_parameters[[sp]]   stage <- names(entry$life_stages)[1]   entry$life_stages[[stage]]$predator$PREDEDEQ %||% NA })  cat("Distribution of PREDEDEQ across database:\n") #> Distribution of PREDEDEQ across database: print(table(prededeq_vals, useNA = "ifany")) #> prededeq_vals #>  1  2  3  #> 61 11  1

what: 7. Building a model directly from the database
title: Exploring the Species Parameter Database
code: # Retrieve brown trout if available, otherwise fall back to first salmonid target_sp <- grep("Salmo trutta|brown trout",                   names(fish4_parameters),                   value = TRUE, ignore.case = TRUE)  if (length(target_sp) == 0) {   target_sp <- onco_keys[1]   # Fall back to first Oncorhynchus } target_sp <- target_sp[1] cat("Using species:", target_sp, "\n") #> Using species: Salmo trutta  entry  <- fish4_parameters[[target_sp]] stage  <- names(entry$life_stages)[1] params <- entry$life_stages[[stage]] info   <- entry$species_info info$life_stage <- stage  # Minimal 60-day setup days_short <- 1:60 bio_db <- Bioenergetic(   species_params     = params,   species_info       = info,   environmental_data = list(     temperature = data.frame(Day = days_short,                              Temperature = 10 + 3 * sin(pi * days_short / 60))   ),   diet_data = list(     proportions = data.frame(Day = days_short, Invertebrates = 1),     prey_names  = "Invertebrates",     energies    = data.frame(Day = days_short, Invertebrates = 2800)   ),   simulation_settings = list(initial_weight = 50, duration = 60) )  # Supply energy density bounds for PREDEDEQ = 1 bio_db$species_params$predator$ED_ini <- 4000 bio_db$species_params$predator$ED_end <- 4500  res_db <- run_fb4(bio_db,                   fit_to    = "Weight",                   fit_value = 80,                   strategy  = "binary_search",                   verbose   = FALSE)  cat(sprintf("Estimated p : %.4f  |  Final weight : %.1f g\n",             res_db$summary$p_value,             res_db$summary$final_weight)) #> Estimated p : 0.6651  |  Final weight : 80.0 g

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: run_fb4() supports four strategies estimating proportion maximum consumption (p): Every strategy takes Bioenergetic object input returns fb4_result object structure.

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: Bioenergetic object used four strategies. represents adult Chinook salmon cohort (Oncorhynchus tshawytscha) 100-day growing season. Observed final weights simulated around target 3 000 g used likelihood bootstrap strategies:
code: data(fish4_parameters)  chinook_db <- fish4_parameters[["Oncorhynchus tshawytscha"]] stage      <- names(chinook_db$life_stages)[1] sp_params  <- chinook_db$life_stages[[stage]] sp_info    <- chinook_db$species_info  days <- 1:100 temp_data <- data.frame(   Day         = days,   Temperature = round(10 + 5 * sin(2 * pi * (days - 60) / 365), 2) ) diet_data <- list(   proportions = data.frame(Day = days, Alewife = 0.7, Shrimp = 0.3),   prey_names  = c("Alewife", "Shrimp"),   energies    = data.frame(Day = days, Alewife = 4900, Shrimp = 3200) )  bio <- Bioenergetic(   species_params      = sp_params,   species_info        = sp_info,   environmental_data  = list(temperature = temp_data),   diet_data           = diet_data,   simulation_settings = list(initial_weight = 1800, duration = 100) ) bio$species_params$predator$ED_ini <- 4500 bio$species_params$predator$ED_end <- 5500 set.seed(42) obs_weights <- rnorm(30, mean = 3000, sd = 80)

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: Binary search finds p produces specified target weight. Use point estimate enough known growth target.
code: res_bs <- run_fb4(bio,                   strategy  = "binary_search",                   fit_to    = "Weight",                   fit_value = 3000)  cat("p estimate  :", round(res_bs$summary$p_value, 4), "\n") #> p estimate  : 0.9095 cat("Final weight:", round(res_bs$summary$final_weight, 1), "g\n") #> Final weight: 3000 g

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: MLE finds p maximises likelihood observing supplied weights, assuming log-normal distribution around predicted weight. returns standard error 95 % confidence interval via profile likelihood.
code:
res_mle <- run_fb4(bio,                    strategy         = "mle",                    fit_to           = "Weight",                    observed_weights = obs_weights)  cat("p estimate :", round(res_mle$summary$p_value, 4), "
") #> p estimate : 0.9106 cat("SE         :", round(res_mle$summary$p_se,    4), "
") #> SE         : 0.0055 cat("Converged  :", res_mle$summary$converged, "
") #> Converged  : TRUE

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: Bootstrap resamples observed weights re-fits model replicate, producing distribution p estimates without assuming particular form weight distribution.
code: res_boot <- run_fb4(bio,                     strategy         = "bootstrap",                     fit_to           = "Weight",                     observed_weights = obs_weights,                     n_bootstrap      = 100,                     upper            = 1,                     parallel         = FALSE)  cat("p mean :", round(res_boot$summary$p_mean, 4), "\n") #> p mean : 0.9111 cat("p SD   :", round(res_boot$summary$p_sd,   4), "\n") #> p SD   : 0.0052

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: hierarchical strategy designed mark-recapture bioenergetics: individual fish tagged, weighed release (initial weight), re-weighed recapture (final weight) fixed monitoring period. model estimates p value individual, pools estimates population-level distribution feeding levels. answers two biological questions: - average feeding level (p) population observed environmental conditions? - variable individual feeding behaviour within population?

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: First use binary search find p consistent bio object (initial weight 1 800 g, 100 days). value becomes centre simulated population distribution.
code: res_ref <- run_fb4(bio,                    strategy  = "binary_search",                    fit_to    = "Weight",                    fit_value = 2500) ref_p <- res_ref$summary$p_value cat("Reference p (target 2 500 g):", round(ref_p, 4), "\n") #> Reference p (target 2 500 g): 0.7528 set.seed(123) n_fish <- 20  # Cohort: individual initial weights varying ± 15 % around 1 800 g initial_weights <- round(runif(n_fish, min = 1800 * 0.85, max = 1800 * 1.15))  # Each fish has its own feeding level drawn from a population distribution # centred on the reference p estimated above (sigma_p = 0.06) true_p <- pmax(0.3, pmin(1.5, rnorm(n_fish, mean = ref_p, sd = 0.06)))  # Simulate final weights: run individual direct simulations + measurement noise final_weights <- vapply(seq_len(n_fish), function(i) {   bio_i <- bio   bio_i$simulation_settings$initial_weight <- initial_weights[i]   res_i <- run_fb4(bio_i, strategy = "direct", p_value = true_p[i])   round(res_i$summary$final_weight * rnorm(1, mean = 1, sd = 0.02), 1) }, numeric(1))  mr_data <- data.frame(   individual_id  = sprintf("fish_%02d", seq_len(n_fish)),   initial_weight = initial_weights,   final_weight   = final_weights )  head(mr_data, 6) #>   individual_id initial_weight final_weight #> 1       fish_01           1685       2620.3 #> 2       fish_02           1956       2720.2 #> 3       fish_03           1751       2565.9 #> 4       fish_04           2007       2790.0 #> 5       fish_05           2038       2686.7 #> 6       fish_06           1555       2581.8

text: data generated theoretical population mean p = 0.753 (binary-search estimate 2 500 g target) SD = 0.06. 20 fish drawn, realised sample mean 0.746 — value mu_p recover. sigma_p reflects true individual variation (SD = 0.06) 2 % measurement noise added final weights.

res_hier <- run_fb4(   x                = bio,   strategy         = "hierarchical",   backend          = "tmb",   fit_to           = "Weight",   observed_weights = mr_data )  cat(sprintf("Population mean p  (mu_p)   : %.4f
", res_hier$summary$mu_p_estimate)) #> Population mean p  (mu_p)   : 0.7447 cat(sprintf("Population SD  p  (sigma_p) : %.4f
", res_hier$summary$sigma_p_estimate)) #> Population SD  p  (sigma_p) : 0.0847 cat(sprintf("Number of individuals       : %d
",   res_hier$summary$n_individuals)) #> Number of individuals       : 20 cat(sprintf("Converged                   : %s
",   res_hier$summary$converged)) #> Converged                   : TRUE

title: Statistical estimation strategies
text: mu_p sigma_p biologically meaningful, study design must satisfy conditions. fish data set must belong species life stage must experienced environmental conditions (temperature, diet). mix life stages fish different habitats, mu_p becomes statistical average clear ecological interpretation. model also assumes fixed monitoring period: every individual tracked n_days. Variable recapture intervals currently supported. statistical side, individual p values modelled log scale, implied population distribution approximately log-normal. guarantees p > 0 works well individual variation moderate. model accepts covariate matrix (covariates_matrix) want explain part variation p body size, sex, tagging location; without covariates, unexplained variation accumulates sigma_p. Finally, hierarchical model requires backend = "tmb" — automatic differentiation via TMB needed mixed-effects likelihood. pure-R backend support strategy.

title: Temperature Sensitivity and Climate Change Scenarios
text: Temperature primary environmental driver fish bioenergetics. metabolic rates scale non-linearly temperature, even modest warming can disproportionate effects growth consumption. vignette uses fb4package : Quantify final weight responds temperature shifts (sensitivity analysis) Project growth outcomes +1 °C, +2 °C, +4 °C warming scenarios Identify thermal performance windows using sensitivity plot Compare sensitivity across two ecologically contrasting species

title: Temperature Sensitivity and Climate Change Scenarios
text: use 120-day summer conditions baseline.
code: # Species parameters (Stewart & Ibarra 1991) sp_params_chinook <- list(   consumption = list(     CEQ = 2, CA = 0.303, CB = -0.275,     CQ = 3.0, CTO = 15.0, CTM = 20.0,     CTL = 24.0, CK1 = 0.1, CK4 = 0.13   ),   respiration = list(     REQ = 1, RA = 0.00264, RB = -0.217,     RQ = 0.06818, RTO = 0.0234, RTM = 0.0,     RTL = 25.0, RK1 = 1.0, RK4 = 0.13, RK5 = 0.0   ),   activity  = list(ACT = 1.0, BACT = 0.0),   sda       = list(SDA = 0.172),   egestion  = list(EGEQ = 1, FA = 0.212, FB = -0.222, FG = 0.631),   excretion = list(EXEQ = 1, UA = 0.0314, UB = 0.58, UG = -0.299),   predator  = list(     PREDEDEQ = 2,     Alpha1 = 4500, Beta1 = 6.0,     Alpha2 = 5500, Beta2 = 2.0,     Cutoff = 250   ) )  days <- 1:120  # Baseline temperature: cool Pacific NW summer temp_base <- data.frame(   Day         = days,   Temperature = round(7 + 6 * sin(pi * (days - 20) / 120), 2) )  # Diet diet_props  <- data.frame(Day = days, Alewife = 0.65, Shrimp = 0.35) prey_energy <- data.frame(Day = days, Alewife = 4900, Shrimp = 3200)  # Build baseline object make_bio <- function(temp_df, sp_params, initial_weight = 5) {   bio <- Bioenergetic(     species_params     = sp_params,     species_info       = list(scientific_name = "Oncorhynchus tshawytscha",                                common_name = "Chinook salmon",                                life_stage  = "juvenile"),     environmental_data = list(temperature = temp_df),     diet_data          = list(       proportions = diet_props,       prey_names  = c("Alewife", "Shrimp"),       energies    = prey_energy     ),     simulation_settings = list(initial_weight = initial_weight, duration = 120)   )   bio }  bio_base <- make_bio(temp_base, sp_params_chinook)  cat(sprintf("Baseline temperature range : %.1f – %.1f °C\n",             min(temp_base$Temperature), max(temp_base$Temperature))) #> Baseline temperature range : 4.1 – 13.0 °C

what: 2. Manual sensitivity analysis across temperature offsets
title: Temperature Sensitivity and Climate Change Scenarios
text: run binary search temperature offset record p required achieve 40 g 120 days (target growth endpoint). p-value required achieve 40 g across temperature offsets Estimated p-value function temperature offset. Interpretation: warmer temperatures, Chinook must feed higher proportion maximum ration achieve growth target, respiration costs increase faster maximum consumption near thermal optimum. ~+3 °C, required p approaches exceeds 1, indicating growth 40 g may become impossible temperatures.
code: offsets   <- c(-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4) target_wt <- 40  results_sens <- lapply(offsets, function(delta) {   temp_shifted <- temp_base   temp_shifted$Temperature <- pmax(1, temp_base$Temperature + delta)    bio_s <- make_bio(temp_shifted, sp_params_chinook)   res   <- tryCatch(     run_fb4(bio_s,             fit_to    = "Weight",             fit_value = target_wt,             strategy  = "binary_search",             verbose   = FALSE),     error = function(e) NULL   )    if (is.null(res)) {     return(data.frame(offset = delta, p_value = NA, final_weight = NA))   }   data.frame(     offset       = delta,     p_value      = round(res$summary$p_value,       4),     final_weight = round(res$summary$final_weight,   1)   ) })  sens_df <- do.call(rbind, results_sens)  knitr::kable(   sens_df,   caption    = paste0("p-value required to achieve ", target_wt,                       " g across temperature offsets"),   col.names  = c("Temp. offset (°C)", "Estimated p", "Final weight (g)") ) plot(sens_df$offset, sens_df$p_value,      type = "b", pch = 19, lwd = 2, col = "steelblue",      xlab = "Temperature offset (°C)",      ylab = "Estimated p-value",      main = "Feeding level required to achieve target weight") abline(v = 0, lty = 2, col = "gray50") grid()

title: Temperature Sensitivity and Climate Change Scenarios
text: plot(bio, type = "sensitivity") runs analysis internally produces formatted figure directly. Built-temperature sensitivity plot. point shows p required achieve target weight given temperature offset.
code: plot(bio_base,      type      = "sensitivity",      temp_offsets = c(-3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4),      fit_to    = "Weight",      fit_value = 40,      colors    = "blue") #> === GROWTH SENSITIVITY ANALYSIS === #> Mode: SEQUENTIAL #> Temperature range: 8 - 18 °C ( 6 values) #> P-value range: 0.3 - 1 ( 8 values) #> Total combinations: 48  #>  #> Processing temperature: 8 °C #> Processing temperature: 10 °C #> Processing temperature: 12 °C #> Processing temperature: 14 °C #> Processing temperature: 16 °C #> Processing temperature: 18 °C #>  #> === ANALYSIS COMPLETE === #> Successful simulations: 48 / 48 ( 100 %) #> Maximum growth rate: 1.768 %/d #> Optimal conditions: 14 °C, 100 % feeding (p = 1.00 )

title: Temperature Sensitivity and Climate Change Scenarios
text: Rather fixing target weight, fix p = 0.65 (estimated field data) ask: final weight change warming scenarios? Projected outcomes p = 0.65 four temperature scenarios Projected final weight climate change scenarios (p fixed 0.65).
code: p_fixed   <- 0.65 scenarios <- c("Baseline" = 0, "+1 °C" = 1, "+2 °C" = 2, "+4 °C" = 4)  scenario_results <- lapply(names(scenarios), function(sc) {   delta <- scenarios[[sc]]   temp_s <- temp_base   temp_s$Temperature <- pmax(1, temp_base$Temperature + delta)    bio_s <- make_bio(temp_s, sp_params_chinook)   res   <- run_fb4(bio_s,                    fit_to    = "p_value",                    fit_value = p_fixed,                    strategy  = "direct_p_value",                    verbose   = FALSE)    if (is.null(res)) return(NULL)    data.frame(     Scenario    = sc,     Delta_T     = delta,     Final_wt_g  = round(res$summary$final_weight,       1),     Consumption = round(res$summary$total_consumption_g, 1)   ) })  scenario_df <- do.call(rbind, scenario_results)  knitr::kable(   scenario_df,   caption   = paste0("Projected outcomes at p = ", p_fixed,                      " under four temperature scenarios"),   col.names = c("Scenario", "Δ T (°C)", "Final weight (g)", "Total consumption (g)") ) bar_cols <- c("steelblue", "gold", "darkorange", "firebrick") bp <- barplot(scenario_df$Final_wt_g,               names.arg = scenario_df$Scenario,               col       = bar_cols,               ylab      = "Final weight (g)",               main      = paste("Growth at p =", p_fixed,                                 "under warming scenarios"),               ylim      = c(0, max(scenario_df$Final_wt_g) * 1.25),               border    = NA)  text(bp, scenario_df$Final_wt_g + 0.5,      labels = paste0(scenario_df$Final_wt_g, " g"),      cex = 0.85, font = 2) grid(nx = NA, ny = NULL, col = "gray85")

title: Temperature Sensitivity and Climate Change Scenarios
text: metabolism–consumption balance defines thermal performance window: temperature range fish can maintain positive growth (p < 1). can derive finding temperature p = 1 target weight. Thermal performance window: p required achieve 40 g 120 days constant temperatures. shaded region shows feasible window (p ≤ 1).
code: # Sweep a fine temperature grid and record required p temp_grid   <- seq(2, 24, by = 1) p_at_target <- sapply(temp_grid, function(t_const) {   temp_const <- data.frame(Day = days, Temperature = t_const)   bio_t <- make_bio(temp_const, sp_params_chinook)   tryCatch({     res <- run_fb4(bio_t,                    fit_to    = "Weight",                    fit_value = target_wt,                    strategy  = "binary_search",                    verbose   = FALSE)     res$summary$p_value   }, error = function(e) NA_real_) })  perf_df <- data.frame(Temperature = temp_grid, p_required = p_at_target) feasible <- perf_df[!is.na(perf_df$p_required) & perf_df$p_required <= 1, ]  cat(sprintf("Feasible temperature range for %.0f g in %d days : %.0f – %.0f °C\n",             target_wt, max(days),             min(feasible$Temperature), max(feasible$Temperature))) #> Feasible temperature range for 40 g in 120 days : 2 – 19 °C plot(perf_df$Temperature, perf_df$p_required,      type = "l", lwd = 2, col = "steelblue",      xlab = "Constant water temperature (°C)",      ylab = "p required to achieve 40 g",      main = "Thermal performance window — juvenile Chinook",      ylim = c(0, max(perf_df$p_required, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.1))  # Shade feasible zone polygon(c(min(feasible$Temperature), feasible$Temperature,           max(feasible$Temperature)),         c(0, feasible$p_required, 0),         col = adjustcolor("steelblue", alpha.f = 0.2),         border = NA)  abline(h = 1, lty = 2, col = "firebrick", lwd = 1.5) text(max(temp_grid) - 2, 1.02, "p = 1 (maximum ration)", col = "firebrick", cex = 0.8) grid()

title: fb4package — Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 in R
text: Comprehensive Energy Budget Modeling: Complete simulation consumption, metabolism, excretion, egestion, growth Multi-Species Support: Built-parameters 105+ fish species models across different life stages Automatic Parameter Fitting: Iterative optimization reach target weights consumption rates using binary search algorithms Environmental Integration: Temperature effects habitat-dependent functions (dissolved oxygen, salinity) Flexible Diet Modeling: Multi-prey diet compositions daily variation indigestible fractions Temporal Dynamics: Daily resolution seasonal environmental variation support Advanced Visualization: Comprehensive plotting functions growth, consumption, energy budgets, diet analysis Research Reproducibility: Scriptable, version-controlled bioenergetics analyses

fb4package — Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 in R
code: library(fb4package)  # Load built-in species parameters data("fish4_parameters", package = "fb4package") chinook_params <- fish4_parameters[["Oncorhynchus tshawytscha"]]  # Create temperature data (seasonal variation) temperature_data <- data.frame(   Day = 1:365,   Temperature = 10 + 6 * sin(2 * pi * (1:365) / 365)  # 4°C ± 6°C seasonal cycle )  # Define diet composition diet_data <- data.frame(   Day = 1:365,   anchoveta = 0.37,  # 37% anchoveta   sardina = 0.63     # 63% sardina )  # Prey energy densities (J/g) prey_energy_data <- data.frame(   Day = 1:365,   anchoveta = 5553,   sardina = 5000 )  # Indigestible fractions indigestible_data <- data.frame(   Day = 1:365,   anchoveta = 0.05,   sardina = 0.05 )  # Create bioenergetic object bio_obj <- Bioenergetic(   species_info = chinook_params$species_info,   species_params = chinook_params$life_stages$adult,   environmental_data = list(temperature = temperature_data),   diet_data = list(     proportions = diet_data,     energies = prey_energy_data,     indigestible = indigestible_data,     prey_names = c("anchoveta", "sardina")   ),   simulation_settings = list(     initial_weight = 11115.358,  # Initial weight (g)     duration = 365               # Simulation days   ) )  # Set predator energy density parameters bio_obj$species_params <- set_parameter_value(bio_obj$species_params, "ED_ini", 6308.570) bio_obj$species_params <- set_parameter_value(bio_obj$species_params, "ED_end", 6320.776)  # Run simulation with automatic fitting to target weight results <- run_fb4(   bio_obj,   fit_to = "Weight",   fit_value = 14883.695,  # Target final weight (g)   max_iterations = 25 )  # View results print(paste("Final weight:", round(results$summary$final_weight, 2), "g")) print(paste("p estimate  :", round(results$summary$p_estimate, 4)))

what: Consumption Functions for FB4 Model — consumption-functions
title: Consumption Functions for FB4 Model — consumption-functions
text: Functions implementing four consumption temperature-dependence equations (CEQ 1–4) allometric maximum-consumption function used FB4. Consumption modelled : $$C = C_{\max} \cdot p \cdot F(T), \quad C_{\max} = CA \cdot W^{CB}$$ \(p\) proportion maximum consumption (P-value), \(F(T)\) temperature-dependence function, \(W\) body mass (g), \(CA\), \(CB\) species-specific intercept slope coefficients. CEQ 1 — simple Q10 exponential: \(F(T) = e^{CQ \cdot T}\) CEQ 2 — Kitchell et al. (1977): \(F(T) = V^{CX} \cdot e^{CX(1-V)}\), \(V = (CTM - T)/(CTM - CTO)\) CEQ 3 — Thornton Lessem (1978): two-part sigmoid using \(CQ\), \(CTO\), \(CTL\), \(CTM\), \(CK1\), \(CK4\) CEQ 4 — polynomial: \(F(T) = e^{CQ \cdot T + CK1 \cdot T^2 + CK4 \cdot T^3}\)

title: Egestion and Excretion Functions for FB4 Model — egestion-excretion
text: Functions implementing four egestion models (EGEQ 1–4) four excretion models (EXEQ 1–4). represent losses consumed energy feces (F) nitrogenous wastes (U) respectively. Egestion models: EGEQ 1 — constant fraction: \(F = FA \cdot C\) EGEQ 2 — Elliott (1976), temperature- feeding-dependent: \(F = FA \cdot T^{FB} \cdot e^{FG \cdot p} \cdot C\) EGEQ 3 — Stewart et al. (1983), includes indigestible fraction: modified form EGEQ 2 accounting indigestible prey material. EGEQ 4 — Elliott (1976), temperature-dependent : \(F = FA \cdot T^{FB} \cdot C\) Excretion models: EXEQ 1 — constant fraction assimilated energy: \(U = UA \cdot (C - F)\) EXEQ 2–3 — Elliott (1976), temperature- feeding-dependent: \(U = UA \cdot T^{UB} \cdot e^{UG \cdot p} \cdot (C - F)\) EXEQ 4 — temperature-dependent .

title: Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 Package — fb4package-package
text: R package implementation Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 (FB4), widely used approach modelling energy allocation fish. model partitions consumed energy three fundamental components: metabolism (respiration + activity + specific dynamic action), waste losses (egestion + excretion), growth (somatic + gonadal). package provides multiple estimation strategies (binary search, direct, bootstrap, maximum likelihood) optional C++ backend via TMB high-performance MLE.

title: Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 Package — fb4package-package
text: bioenergetics energy balance implemented follows Kitchell et al. (1977): $$C = R + + \mathrm{SDA} + F + U + G$$ \(C\) = consumption, \(R\) = standard respiration, \(\) = active metabolism, \(\mathrm{SDA}\) = specific dynamic action, \(F\) = egestion, \(U\) = excretion, \(G\) = growth (somatic + gonadal). Consumption modelled \(C = C_{\max} \cdot p \cdot F(T)\), \(C_{\max} = CA \cdot W^{CB}\) (Hartman Hayward 2007) \(F(T)\) one four temperature-dependence functions (CEQ 1–4; Kitchell et al. 1977; Thornton Lessem 1978). Respiration modelled \(R = RA \cdot W^{RB} \cdot F(T) \cdot \mathrm{ACT}\), \(F(T)\) following Kitchell et al. (1977) simple Q10 exponential (REQ 1–2). Egestion excretion follow Elliott (1976) Stewart et al. (1983) (EGEQ/EXEQ 1–4). Predator energy density can modelled weight-dependent constant (PREDEDEQ 1–2; Hanson et al. 1997). package extends Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 (Deslauriers et al. 2017), R-based re-implementation Fish Bioenergetics 3.0 (Hanson et al. 1997) predecessors (Hewett Johnson 1987, 1992).

title: Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 Package — fb4package-package
text: Deslauriers, D., Chipps, S.R., Breck, J.E., Rice, J.. Madenjian, C.P. (2017). Fish Bioenergetics 4.0: R-based modeling application. Fisheries, 42(11), 586–596. doi:10.1080/03632415.2017.1377558 Hanson, P.C., Johnson, T.B., Schindler, D.E. Kitchell, J.F. (1997). Fish Bioenergetics 3.0. University Wisconsin Sea Grant Institute, Madison, WI. WISCU-T-97-001. Kitchell, J.F., Stewart, D.J. Weininger, D. (1977). Applications bioenergetics model yellow perch (Perca flavescens) walleye (Stizostedion vitreum vitreum). Journal Fisheries Research Board Canada, 34(10), 1922–1935. Thornton, K.W. Lessem, .S. (1978). temperature algorithm modifying biological rates. Transactions American Fisheries Society, 107(2), 284–287. Elliott, J.M. (1976). Energy losses waste products brown trout (Salmo trutta L.). Journal Animal Ecology, 45(2), 561–580. Stewart, D.J., Weininger, D., Rottiers, D.V. Edsall, T.. (1983). energetics model lake trout, Salvelinus namaycush: application Lake Michigan population. Canadian Journal Fisheries Aquatic Sciences, 40(6), 681–698. Hartman, K.J. Hayward, R.S. (2007). Bioenergetics. C.S. Guy M.L. Brown (eds.), Analysis Interpretation Freshwater Fisheries Data. American Fisheries Society, Bethesda, MD. Hewett, S.W. Johnson, B.L. (1987). Generalized Bioenergetics Model Fish Growth Microcomputers. University Wisconsin Sea Grant Institute, Madison, WI. Hewett, S.W. Johnson, B.L. (1992). Fish Bioenergetics 2.0. University Wisconsin Sea Grant Institute, Madison, WI. Winberg, G.G. (1956). Rate metabolism food requirements fishes. Fisheries Research Board Canada Translation Series . 194.

what: Bootstrap method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_bootstrap
title: Bootstrap method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_bootstrap
text: Propagates p-value uncertainty consumption predictions using parametric bootstrap. Generates multiple samples p-value distribution runs FB4 simulations sample. Provides full uncertainty distribution without linearity assumptions. Supports parallel processing improved performance.

title: Bootstrap method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_bootstrap
code: predict_consumption_bootstrap(   p_mean,   p_sd,   bio_obj,   n_sims = 1000,   first_day = 1,   last_day = 365,   parallel = FALSE,   n_cores = NULL,   confidence_level = 0.95,   verbose = FALSE )

title: Bootstrap method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_bootstrap
text: p_mean Mean p-value distribution p_sd Standard deviation p-value distribution bio_obj Bioenergetic object simulation settings environmental data n_sims Number bootstrap simulations, default 1000 first_day First simulation day, default 1 last_day Last simulation day, default 365 parallel Use parallel processing, default FALSE n_cores Number cores parallel processing (NULL = auto-detect), default NULL confidence_level Confidence level intervals, default 0.95 verbose Show progress messages, default FALSE

title: Bootstrap method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_bootstrap
text: bootstrap method: 1. Samples p-values Normal(p_mean, p_sd) 2. Constrains samples valid range [0.01, 5.0] 3. Runs FB4 simulation p-value sample 4. Summarizes consumption distribution Parallel processing can significantly reduce computation time large n_sims. method handles simulation failures gracefully reports success rates.

title: Bootstrap method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_bootstrap
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Bootstrap uncertainty propagation uncertainty_result <- predict_consumption_bootstrap(   p_mean = 0.8,   p_sd = 0.15,   bio_obj = bio_obj,   n_sims = 1000,   parallel = TRUE )  # Compare with delta method delta_result <- predict_consumption_delta(0.8, 0.15, bio_obj) } # }

what: Delta method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_delta
title: Delta method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_delta
text: Propagates p-value uncertainty consumption predictions using delta method. Computes numerical derivatives applies first-order approximation uncertainty propagation. Suitable relationship p consumption approximately linear.

title: Delta method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_delta

title: Delta method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_delta
text: p_est Estimated p-value (feeding level parameter) p_se Standard error p-value estimate bio_obj Bioenergetic object simulation settings environmental data delta_size Small increment numerical derivative computation, default 0.001 first_day First simulation day, default 1 last_day Last simulation day, default 365 verbose Show progress messages, default FALSE

title: Delta method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_delta
text: delta method uses first-order Taylor series approximation: Var(f(X)) ≈ [f'(μ)]² × Var(X) linearity check verifies derivative times delta_size small relative consumption estimate, indicating local linearity.

title: Delta method for consumption uncertainty propagation — predict_consumption_delta

what: Respiration Functions for FB4 Model — respiration-functions
title: Respiration Functions for FB4 Model — respiration-functions
text: Functions implementing two respiration temperature-dependence equations (REQ 1–2), activity correction, conversion oxygen consumption energy units. Respiration modelled : $$R = RA \cdot W^{RB} \cdot F(T) \cdot \mathrm{ACT}$$ \(RA\) \(RB\) species-specific intercept slope coefficients, \(W\) body mass (g), \(F(T)\) temperature function, \(\mathrm{ACT}\) activity multiplier. REQ 1 — simple Q10 exponential activity: \(F(T) = e^{RQ \cdot T}\); velocity-based activity Kitchell et al. (1977). REQ 2 — Kitchell et al. (1977): \(F(T) = V^{RX} \cdot e^{RX(1-V)}\), \(V = (RTM - T)/(RTM - RTO)\). Oxygen consumption converted energy using oxycalorific coefficient (default 13 560 J g\(^{-1}\) O\(_2\); Elliott Davison 1975).

title: Respiration Functions for FB4 Model — respiration-functions
text: Kitchell, J.F., Stewart, D.J. Weininger, D. (1977). Applications bioenergetics model yellow perch walleye. Journal Fisheries Research Board Canada, 34(10), 1922–1935. Elliott, J.M. Davison, W. (1975). Energy equivalents oxygen consumption animal energetics. Oecologia, 19(3), 195–201. Hanson, P.C., Johnson, T.B., Schindler, D.E. Kitchell, J.F. (1997). Fish Bioenergetics 3.0. University Wisconsin Sea Grant Institute, Madison, WI. Deslauriers, D., Chipps, S.R., Breck, J.E., Rice, J.. Madenjian, C.P. (2017). Fish Bioenergetics 4.0: R-based modeling application. Fisheries, 42(11), 586–596. doi:10.1080/03632415.2017.1377558

title: Temperature function for respiration - Equation 2 (Low-level) — respiration_temp_eq2

fb4package 2.0.0 (2026-03-28)
text: major rewrite. entire codebase reorganised monolithic structure (7 files) modular, strategy-based architecture (33 files), optional TMB/C++ backend added statistical estimation methods.

title: fb4package 2.0.0 (2026-03-28)
text: Added optional TMB (Template Model Builder) backend maximum likelihood hierarchical estimation, providing 10–50× speedup pure-R implementation (src/fb4_main.cpp). New C++ headers consumption, respiration, egestion/excretion, body composition, validation (src/includes/). Added Rcpp, TMB (>= 1.7.0), RcppEigen dependencies. R (>= 4.0.0) now required (>= 3.5.0).

title: fb4package 2.0.0 (2026-03-28)
text: "optim" — gradient-based fitting stats::optim(). "mle" — maximum likelihood estimation p-value SE 95% CI, using observed final weights (observed_weights). "bootstrap" — non-parametric uncertainty quantification via resampling observed final weights. Supports parallel execution (future/furrr). "hierarchical" — population-level mixed-effects model estimating mean SD p across individuals (TMB required).

title: fb4package 2.0.0 (2026-03-28)
text: Simulation engine extracted R/12-simulation-engine.R. Fitting algorithms split individual strategy files (14.2.1 14.2.6), shared commons layer (14.0.1-strategy-commons.R), strategy interface (14.1). Validation split four specialised modules (11.0-core, 11.1-basic, 11.2-parameter, 11.3-data, 11.4-main). Bioenergetic class methods separated 13.0-bioenergetic-classes.R 13.1-bioenergetic-methods.R. Analysis layer split five focused modules (15.0–15.4). Plot layer split four modules (16.1–16.4) covering core helpers, daily output plots, analysis plots, bioenergetic-object plots. Result builders centralised 14.3-result-builders.R consistent field names (p_value, total_consumption_g, converged) across strategies.

title: fb4package 0.1.0
text: Initial release. Pure-R implementation Fish Bioenergetics 4.0 (Deslauriers et al. 2017). Binary search fitting (strategy = "binary_search") direct p-value simulation (strategy = "direct"). Bioenergetic S3 class print(), summary(), plot(), run_fb4() methods. Species parameter database 105 models (73 species). compiled code; depends base R packages.
